scraper/ttmp/2026/03/23/SCRAPER-DASHBOARD--dashboard-ui-workflow-monitoring-engine-health-rate-limiting-operations/design-doc/01-dashboard-architecture-and-component-design.md:954:### Polling vs WebSocket
scraper/ttmp/2026/03/23/SCRAPER-DASHBOARD--dashboard-ui-workflow-monitoring-engine-health-rate-limiting-operations/design-doc/01-dashboard-architecture-and-component-design.md:958:RTK Query's `pollingInterval` provides cache-aware polling out of the box. The scraper engine is SQLite-backed with no built-in pub/sub, so a WebSocket would require a separate event bus in the Go server. Polling at 3-5 second intervals is sufficient for workflow monitoring and matches the worker's own poll interval.

scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-RUNTIME-EVENTS--runtime-event-pipeline-for-worker-server-and-frontend/index.md:39:The design comparison is now done. The ticket treats Watermill as the standard eventing layer, keeps the current worker/server split as the default topology, and uses a protobuf-generated event contract shared between Go and TypeScript. Redis-backed transport remains the cross-process path, with in-process transport for tests and optional local mode.

scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-RUNTIME-EVENTS--runtime-event-pipeline-for-worker-server-and-frontend/reference/01-investigation-diary.md:238:The next user message removed the last framework ambiguity: Watermill should be the standardized way we handle the event pipeline. That did not change the topology recommendation. It changed the implementation stance. Instead of treating Watermill as optional, the ticket now treats Watermill as the standard messaging layer while still keeping the worker/server split and Redis-backed cross-process delivery.
scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-RUNTIME-EVENTS--runtime-event-pipeline-for-worker-server-and-frontend/reference/01-investigation-diary.md:240:I kept one important architectural boundary in place while updating the docs. Scraper still needs its own `RuntimeEvent` model. Watermill should carry scraper events, not become the business-level event schema itself. That keeps the frontend contract and the log/artifact policy owned by scraper rather than leaked from the transport library.

That changes an important detail in the design: the event envelope should not just be a scraper-owned Go struct wrapped around Watermill. It should be a scraper-owned protobuf schema that generates both backend and frontend types from one source of truth.
scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-RUNTIME-EVENTS--runtime-event-pipeline-for-worker-server-and-frontend/reference/01-investigation-diary.md:309:That fits the architecture well. Watermill still handles messaging. Protobuf now owns the event contract. The HTTP/SSE boundary becomes `protojson`, and the frontend decodes with generated TS schema support instead of hand-maintained interfaces.

scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-FRONTEND-RUNTIME-EVENTS--frontend-runtime-event-surfaces-for-operators/reference/01-investigation-diary.md:624:- Richer payload rendering improved both the new drawer tab and the existing global event console at the same time, because both reuse `RuntimeEventList.tsx`.
scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-FRONTEND-RUNTIME-EVENTS--frontend-runtime-event-surfaces-for-operators/reference/01-investigation-diary.md:637:The key design correction was on the frontend data boundary. RTK Query had been caching decoded protobuf `RuntimeEventV1` messages. That looked convenient, but the generated timestamp shape contains `bigint` seconds, which Redux Toolkit correctly warns about as non-serializable. The right fix was to keep raw JSON in the RTK cache and decode protobuf messages only in local hook state inside `useRuntimeEventFeed`. That preserves the shared hook design while keeping Redux state serializable.

scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-FRONTEND-RUNTIME-EVENTS--frontend-runtime-event-surfaces-for-operators/design-doc/01-frontend-runtime-event-surfaces-architecture-and-intern-implementation-guide.md:43:    - Path: web/src/components/workflows/RuntimeEventList.tsx
scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-FRONTEND-RUNTIME-EVENTS--frontend-runtime-event-surfaces-for-operators/design-doc/01-frontend-runtime-event-surfaces-architecture-and-intern-implementation-guide.md:75:The runtime event backend pipeline now exists. The worker, submission path, and HTTP server all publish `RuntimeEventV1` protobuf messages, the API server stores a bounded recent history in memory, and the frontend already renders one workflow-local timeline. That means the hard platform work is done. The remaining job is product work: turn the raw event stream into operator-facing frontend surfaces that are easy to understand, safe to extend, and consistent with the rest of the application.
scraper/ttmp/2026/03/24/SCRAPER-FRONTEND-RUNTIME-EVENTS--frontend-runtime-event-surfaces-for-operators/design-doc/01-frontend-runtime-event-surfaces-architecture-and-intern-implementation-guide.md:77:Today the web app has only one runtime event surface: the workflow detail page fetches recent events for a workflow and opens an `EventSource` stream for live updates in `web/src/pages/WorkflowDetailPage.tsx:74-123`. That is a good first integration, but it is intentionally narrow. There is no global event console, no op-scoped runtime event tab, no reusable streaming hook, no connection-status UI, and no dashboard widgets that turn live events into operational signals.

That meant the registry was empty at construction time, but scraper's dynamic site verbs are built when `newSiteCommand(...)` iterates the loaded registry. The user explicitly wanted to support multiple site directories from flags, env, and config, so I stopped and wrote down the bootstrap pattern before changing code.

scraper/ttmp/2026/04/08/SCRAPER-DECLARATIVE-SITES--manifest-driven-site-loading-with-js-first-site-definitions/reference/01-investigation-diary.md:646:With the app-config layer in place, I implemented the actual bootstrap path that scraper's real binary needs. The important behavior change is that `cmd/scraper/main.go` no longer constructs the root command directly from `NewRootCommand(version)`. It now resolves manifest directories first ŌĆö from config, environment, and bootstrap CLI flags ŌĆö and only then builds the Cobra tree. That finally aligns scraper with the sqleton pattern that inspired this work.

Each retry changes the `recentRuntimeEvents` reference, triggering the merge effect, which triggers a re-render, which triggers another RTK Query evaluation.

RTK Query's built-in `onCacheEntryAdded` lifecycle manages SSE natively: no local state, no effect chains, auto-cleanup on unsubscribe.

scraper/ttmp/2026/04/07/UI-001--ui-redesign-runtime-events-workflow-detail-and-system-ux-improvements/analysis/01-bug-analysis-runtimeeventfeed-infinite-loop.md:92:The key insight: **`recentRuntimeEvents` is an array reference that changes on every RTK Query cache update, even when empty.** The effect that merges it into `allEvents` calls `setAllEvents` on every change, which triggers a re-render, which can cause RTK Query to re-evaluate its cache state.
scraper/ttmp/2026/04/07/UI-001--ui-redesign-runtime-events-workflow-detail-and-system-ux-improvements/analysis/01-bug-analysis-runtimeeventfeed-infinite-loop.md:108:| `recentRuntimeEvents` defaults to `[]` | Even a failed query returns `[]` as the default, which is a new array reference each render |

scraper/ttmp/2026/04/07/UI-001--ui-redesign-runtime-events-workflow-detail-and-system-ux-improvements/analysis/02-bug-report-msw-handlers-not-intercepting-rtk-query-requests-in-storybook.md:22:Summary: 'MSW handlers were not intercepting RTK Query fetch requests in Storybook. Continued investigation confirmed the primary root cause is a dependency incompatibility: msw-storybook-addon 2.0.6 assumes worker.context exists, but msw 2.13.0 removed that field from setupWorker() results. The bug is now fixed locally by pinning msw to 2.12.0, serializing runtime event mocks through real Buf messages, disabling serializable checks in Storybook stores, and skipping SSE in Storybook so the unmocked stream endpoint does not 404 noisily.'
